item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this as an SCP and it is well deserving of an up vote. However I found out someone has made a mobile game about this SCP that makes the Foundation sound worse than in CB. Found it on the Apple App Store while looking for SCP apps a while back. Has anyone played this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a lot of SCP-140, but it's different enough to earn an upvote. Definitely think that this deserves a bit more attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am so glad I found this in the "underrated" thread because it is super great. Good job with the infohazard concept and the idea of the story being hard-wired into their very genes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm fond of this one. It seems like it's almost retro-causal, or meant to appear retro-causal. That kind of mechanic has always fascinated me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
im imagining a bunch of tribe-members ranting about how their version is the real one and repeatedly telling it.

xk-event
vk-event
xxxk-event
kk-event
whateverwearescrewedanywaysk-event

all of them

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I got was that it appeared in the vicinity of the storyteller. The reason it's always in the site is because the story is only being told within the site.

I do slightly prefer it without the link to missing people being confirmed as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if this has been clarified or if I just missed something in the article, but does 1625-1 show up in the same general location each time except with changes in how the body was interred? It seems like the case, since there doesn't appear to be any difficulty in finding it, but at the same time it wasn't clear to me.

Also not sure if you need the link to various missing persons around the world- it's just as interesting thinking that the bodies are of different people in the past who were 1625-1, but that might be a personal preference thing.

Overall delaying my vote for the moment but the concept is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like, the "laughing is contagious" but laughing is replaced with story-telling. I think. It's hard to explain this with in my own words, and it confounds me. I love the idea, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I hoped that it would add to the idea. I imagine people are taken away in the night by something, and forced to relive the life of the ethnarch, right up until his death, however violent it may be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this concept, though the exact details of 1625-1 were a little confusing. That said, I wasn't going to upvote until the source of 1625-1 was revealed; that's a great little detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If anybody wants to fix up any grammar or awkward wording, feel free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is that once you know of it, you can infect someone else. So, yes, if you keep quiet, you won't spread it.

There is only one instance of SCP-1625-1 at any given time, but a new one shows up the second 1625 is transferred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. A keter which is that because it's hard to contain rather than dangerous, and at least on first read, everything looks A-OK in procedures etc. as well.
And there's the thing with either just generating a corpse or altering history…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely better with the edits. It's still a little unclear with the contagiousness of it. My understanding is that if a member of the tribe tells it to me, I'm now infected. If I tell it to someone else, they're now infected, and so on. Correct?

And there is no compulsion to actually tell the story to anyone, right? So if I know I've been infected, I just keep my mouth shut and no new instance of 1625-1 occurs? And are there multiple copies of 1625-1, or just one at any given time?

Improved enough to be an upvote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, this is a genius concept right here. It needs proofreading, though; I spotted multiple spelling errors, for starters. Also going to concur that this thing seriously needs to be Keter. Upvoting tentatively, but hoping to see this article polished to the shine it deserves soon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No upvote yet, because of issues already discussed. Will probably upvote enthusiastically after revisions. The ethnography is quite good, and you did your homework, which is surprising — most social science SCPs are sort of shoddy in that regard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirding this, but I really like the concept, so…upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Drew basically summed up my feelings on the matter. I'm going to tentatively upvote this because it answered most of what needed answering, and left just enough WTFery to be enticing, but those couple of issues stand out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is… interesting. And it definitely has potential. But unless I'm misunderstanding something, anyone can pass on the story once they've been "infected" with it, right? Or is it only if they were told/exposed to the story originally from one of the tribe? I.e., would the exposed researcher be able to infect someone else with his story? Because if that's the case, this is clearly a Keter due to the INCREDIBLE difficulty in containing it. If the infectious story has to originate from a member of Tribe A, then it's somewhat easier to contain, and I agree that Euclid would be more appropriate.

Also, I can't tell: are the members of the tribe physically separated from each other in order to prevent them from telling each other the story? If not, why not? And why are we keeping them at the original tribal location? Why not move them to a more secure facility (again with the separation in private containment suites), and monitor the tribal location separately?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd get rid of the "A" designation and either use a real tribal group or blackbox it out. "A" doesn't mean anything. Other than that, it reads well enough to me, although some people will be put off by all the black boxes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept you're working from, but the execution leaves a lot to be desired. You should really read over it a few more times and condense the language a bit. As it stands, it's pretty confusingly written. I'd also look at the formatting and get rid of much of the expungement in the containment procedures (only the date could probably remain blackboxed). For now it's -1 from me, but I'm watching the page, so if you tackle the stuff that irks me, I'd be happy to change my mind.

item.title: New Entry
item.content:encoded: This is my newest attempt, added after some work in the sandbox and feedback on the forum. I hope it's well received, and I appreciate thoughts on it!
